coredata.dc:title: Chapter 7 Fluorescent Graphene Quantum Dots for the Determination of Metal Ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Novel Nanomaterials for Biomedical, Environmental and Energy Applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Feiming
Lin, Liping
Chen, Xi
coredata.dc:description: This chapter presents recent progress on the determination of metal ions in aqueous solution using not only fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) but also heteroatom-doped GQDs and surface-functionalized GQDs. The analytic performances of the methods using different types of GQDs are critically discussed. In addition, some limitations including sensitivity, selectivity, and variety of the metal ions detected are also discussed. Finally, future research work searching for highly fluorescent GQDs as probes for the sensitive and selective determination of metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+) is recommended.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent determination
Metal ions
Synthesis
Probes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical Application of GQDs for the Determination of Metal Ions

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the preparation of GQDs1 and GQDs2 from SWCNTs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Dong Y, Pang H, Ren S, Chen C, Chi Y, Yu T. Etching single-wall carbon nanotubes into green and yellow single-layer graphene quantum dots. Carbon 2013;64:245–51. Copyright © 2013 Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process for GQDs. (A) Steps involved in the preparation of near-UV-emitting GQDs, with associated photographs and schematic diagrams. The photograph of the photoluminescent reduced graphene oxide (rGO) hydrogel in hexane was obtained under excitation at 365nm. (B) Model of a portion of the hydrogel in water, with GQDs adsorbed on the surface of large rGO sheets using π-π stacking interactions. (C) Model of a portion of the hydrogel in an organic solvent, with the photoluminescent GQDs exfoliated from the surface of the rGO sheet by the organic solvent. (D and E) Photographs of GQDs dispersed in different organic solvents: (from left to right) tetrahydrofuran, acetone, ethanol, dichloromethane, ether, chloroform, toluene, and hexane. Photographs were taken under (D) visible light and (E) UV light (365nm).

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Qin H, Gong T, Jin Y, Cho Y, Shin C, Lee C, et al. Near-UV-emitting graphene quantum dots from graphene hydrogels. Carbon 2015;94:181–8. Copyright © 2015 Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process. (A) Graphitic N-GQDs from in situ annealing and electroexfoliation of pencil beads. N-doped porous pencil beads were first obtained by adsorption of melamine molecules and in situ annealing, and the graphitic N-GQDs were then prepared using a process of electroexfoliation. (B) Photographs of time-dependent color changes of the ultrasmall graphitic pencil nanodot solutions.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Kong B, Tang J, Zhang Y, Jiang T, Gong X, Peng C, et al. Incorporation of well-dispersed sub-5-nm graphitic pencil nanodots into ordered mesoporous frameworks. Nat Chem 2016;8:171–8.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photograph of a typical microfluidizer. The graphite aqueous suspension is powered using a 30kpsi pump from the feed tank through micron-sized channels. Schematics of (B) Z-shaped channels with diameters ranging from 400μm down to 87μm and (C) a typical flow profile within the channel with a maximal flow speed of 400m/s. The graphite flakes are exfoliated into graphene sheets and further fragmented into nanosized GQDs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Buzaglo M, Shtein M, Regev O. Graphene quantum dots produced by microfluidization. Chem Mater 2016;28:21–4.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQDs and graphene nanoribbons (GRs) derived from anthracite using supercritical fluids as swelling and oxidizing/reducing media.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Sasikala SP, Henry L, Yesilbag Tonga G, Huang K, Das R, Giroire B, et al. High yield synthesis of aspect ratio controlled graphenic materials from anthracite coal in supercritical fluids. ACS Nano 2016;10:5293–303.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of GQDs using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Tang L, Ji R, Cao X, Lin J, Jiang H, Li X, Teng KS, Luk CM, Zeng S, Hao J, Lau SP. Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots. ACS Nano 2014;6:5102–10.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of GQDs and GO. The black dots in the GO represent oxygen atoms.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Dong Y, Shao J, Chen C, Li H, Wang R, Chi Y, et al. Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid. Carbon 2012;50:4738–43. Copyright © 2012 Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram illustrating the under pinning mechanism of switching.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Yang S, Sun J, He P, Deng X, Wang Z, Hu C, et al. Selenium doped graphene quantum dots as an ultrasensitive redox fluorescent switch. Chem Mater 2015;27:2004–11.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The structure of the ratiometric fluorescence probe and its working principle for Hg2+ determination; fluorescence spectra and the corresponding fluorescence colors (λex=365nm) of the as-obtained (B) CdTe@SiO2@GQD ratiometric probes; (C) GQDs probes.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Hua M, Wang C, Qian J, Wang K, Yang Z, Liu Q, et al. Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury (II) ions. Anal Chim Acta 2015;888:173–81. Copyright © 2015 Elsevier).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of a five-layered sample pretreatment microdevice. The microdevice consists of a glass manifold, a PDMS thin film, a glass microchannel layer, a PDMS chamber layer, and a glass bottom layer. (B) Digital image of the cation exchange resin incorporated microdevice (top) and a side view of an assembled microdevice (bottom). (C) Experimental scheme for Pb2+ detection on a GQD sensor through electron-transfer-mediated fluorescence quenching.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Park M, Ha HD, Kim YT, Jung JH, Kim SH, Kim do H, et al. Combination of a sample pretreatment microfluidic device with a photoluminescent graphene oxide quantum dot sensor for trace lead detection. Anal Chem 2015;87:10969–75.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7.11

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the catalytic effect of N-GQDs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Zhang L, Peng D, Liang RP, Qiu JD. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a new catalyst accelerating the coordination reaction between cadmium (II) and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin for cadmium (II) sensing. Anal Chem 2015;87:10894–901.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Brief Summary of GQD-Based Sensors for the Detection of Metal Ions

row.entry: Wastewater, river water, and lake water
Drinking water, tap water, and lake water
Tap water and real river water
Tap water, well water, human plasma
GO prepared from natural graphite and ammonium hydroxide

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 7
chapter.ce:title: Fluorescent Graphene Quantum Dots for the Determination of Metal Ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This chapter presents recent progress on the determination of metal ions in aqueous solution using not only fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) but also heteroatom-doped GQDs and surface-functionalized GQDs. The analytic performances of the methods using different types of GQDs are critically discussed. In addition, some limitations including sensitivity, selectivity, and variety of the metal ions detected are also discussed. Finally, future research work searching for highly fluorescent GQDs as probes for the sensitive and selective determination of metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+) is recommended.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Nature Scientific Foundation of China (No. 21375112). We sincerely thank Professor John Hodgkiss of the City University of Hong Kong for his assistance with English.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are ubiquitous in the environmental and biological fields. Some metal ions are essential to many organisms. For example, alkali and alkaline earth metal ions (such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+) [1–4] play the most important role in human physiological activities, whereas high doses of ions affect the ecosystem and human health, especially the toxic metal ions (e.g., Pb2+ [5], Hg2+ [6], and As3+ [7]) even at small doses. Therefore, it is one of the important themes for the analyst to pursue in order to develop a powerful technique for the determination of metal ions. Currently, there have been various detection assays including voltammetry [8,9], colorimetric assay [10], atomic absorption spectrometry [5,11], Raman spectrometry [12,13], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7,14–16]. However, most of these methods require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures, which limit their practical applications. Recently, fluorescence assays using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely established with high sensitivity, great simplicity, easy monitoring, and rapid response and provide a better choice for the detection of metal ions beyond traditional fluorescent probes such as organic chromophores [17,18], fluorescent conjugated polymers [19,20], metal clusters [6,21], and semiconductor quantum dots [22,23].

GQDs are two-dimensional graphene nanosheets in the form of one, two, or more layers less than 10nm thick and 100nm in lateral size [24], and they exhibit exciton confinement and quantum-size effects with an intriguing excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL). In addition, GQDs exhibit good biocompatibility, stable PL, resistance to photobleaching, tunable luminescence emission, and high solubility in various solvents [25–30]. Moreover, there are various functional groups (such as carboxyl, epoxide, carbonyl, and hydroxyl) at the edges of GQDs, which can not only act as reaction sites but also alter their electronic and optical properties by doping or functionalization [31–33]. By virtue of these exceptional properties, GQDs have been widely used in sensing [33], organic photovoltaic devices [32], photocatalysis [34,35], bioimaging [36,37], DNA cleavage [38,39], and terahertz bolometers [40], which demonstrates their bright prospects.

In this chapter, we attempt to provide a critical overview for a better understanding of the synthesis, doping, and functionalization of GQDs. Furthermore, the applications of GQDs for the determination of ions are summarized and commented upon. The roles of dopants and surface functional groups of GQDs in the determination of metal ions are also discussed in order to extensively elicit more thought and constructive attempts toward the sensing of metal ions.

There are two basic approaches for synthesizing GQDs, which have traditionally been classified into the top-down and bottom-up methods [31,33]. The major routes to produce GQDs in both paradigms will be discussed to identify recent advances and highlight the benefits and drawbacks of these routes.

In a typical top-down approach, the GQDs are restructured or “broken off” from larger macroscopic carbon materials, including carbon nanotubes [41,42], graphene oxide (GO) [4,43,44], coal [45,46], carbon fibers [47], graphite nanoparticles or rods [48,49], 3D graphene [50,51], and metal-organic framework-derived porous carbon [52]. These approaches consist of acid-based chemical treatment (sonication and reflux) [41,42,50–52], hydrothermal [53,54], solvothermal [55,56], electrochemical treatment [48,49], laser ablation or exfoliation [4,43,44], mechanical microfluidization [57], and supercritical fluid treatment [58].

In an acid-based oxidation procedure, bulk materials are cut into GQDs using one or more concentrated strong acids (e.g., nitric acid or mixtures of nitric and sulfuric acids) in an oxidation process. A facile method for the preparation of GQDs using acidic oxidation of pitch carbon fibers in a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 under stirring for 24h is reported, and the size of GQDs can be successfully controlled just by using different reaction temperatures [47]. Here, three types of GQDs with diameter distributions in the ranges 1–4, 4–8, and 7–11nm are produced at 120°C, 100°C, and 80°C, and the fluorescence wavelength of the GQDs present redshifts from blue to yellow as a result of the quantum confinement and edge effects [47]. A three-step method has also been developed to produce single-layer GQDs from single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), as shown in Fig. 7.1 , where two types of GQDs (GQDs1 and GQDs2) with green and yellow PL were obtained by controlling the concentration of HNO3 [41]. Both GQDs are single-layered nanosheets with an average lateral size of 8nm and an average height of 0.5nm with excellent monodispersity in water. Further, unlike the long treatment time in the acid-based oxidation processes as mentioned above, a microwave-assisted acid-based oxidation approach for the synthesis of greenish yellow luminescent GQDs from GO nanosheets under an acid condition (3.2M HNO3 and 0.9M H2SO4) has been developed, and the microwave irradiation offers a rapid and uniform heating condition, which dramatically reduces the reaction time to 3h [59].

Hydrothermal and solvothermal routes are both widely used for GQD synthesis. These methods involve dissolving or dispersing appropriate carbon-based raw materials in water or organic solvents (e.g., dimethylformamide, toluene, and hexane) and heating at high pressure in an autoclave [53–56]. Although the hydrothermal approach is a relatively simple method, the PL of the obtained GQDs often suffers from quenching due to the large graphene nanoparticles in the resulting GQD colloids [56]. Purification of the GQDs after hydrothermal cutting becomes necessary [53], and the process is time-consuming. Based on this fact, a novel, facile, and “slow-release” method to prepare near-UV-emitting GQDs from graphene hydrogels, which is another form and derivative of graphene, was developed [56]. These GQDs display a blue fluorescence emission in the near-UV region (Fig. 7.2 ); the method does not require such posttreatments as centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis, and the process can be expanded to construct doped GQDs when dopants are introduced.

Electrochemical synthesis of GQDs was first demonstrated when an electrochemical cell was built using a multiwalled carbon nanotube-modified working electrode [60]. Alternatively, GQDs can be obtained by electrolyzing a graphite rod [30,61], GO film [62], and other graphitic carbon-based raw materials [63]. In addition, doped GQDs can be obtained using doped raw materials or varying the electrolyte, such as sodium p-toluenesulfonate [48] and borax (99.5%) [61]. Recently, N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have been electrochemically produced using N-doped pencil beads (N-PBs) as the anode and the cathode in NaOH/ethanol electrolyte [63]. As illustrated in Fig. 7.3 , when potential is applied with a current intensity of ~ 50mAcm−2, the solution color changes from colorless to yellow and then dark brown, indicating that the GQDs are exfoliated from the N-PBs.

The limitations of these existing top-down approaches are that they need preoxidation steps with harsh reaction conditions [53,59] and tedious post synthesis purification procedures in order to improve the product purity (generally from 24 to 48h) [30,48,61]. In general, the GQDs obtained are of broad size distribution and low product yield. What is worse is that the introduction of internal stress and faults in the crystallographic network leads to surface defects and structural damage and ultimately alters the surface properties owing to the typically larger surface area per unit volume of these materials [53,59,62]. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop a scalable method that can be carried out under milder reaction conditions and without any complicated procedures.

Recently, a purely mechanical microfluidization method was developed to produce nonfunctionalized GQDs that are free of chemically bound species on their surface [57]. Fig. 7.4A depicts this microfluidization method for the production of GQDs. Graphite water suspensions are powered by a high-pressure pump through microsized Z-shaped channels (Fig. 7.4B) to exfoliate graphite flakes into graphene sheets and after that to fragment them into nanosized GQDs (Fig. 7.4C). However, despite the remarkable improvement in the synthesis of GQDs using the microfluidization method, the low PL quantum yield (PLQY) (1.32%) of this technology remains a concern for large-scale implementation.

Recently, a more effective and green alternative process to obtain GQDs was put forward [58]. Since coal possesses an inherently disordered structure compared with that of graphite, it is easier to be exfoliated. In this process, supercritical fluid treatment is used for the preparation of GQDs, with the whole procedure being carried out in an environmentally friendly way without any preoxidation or posttreatment purification, making it possible to scale up for mass production. Graphene nanoribbons can also be produced when supercritical ethanol is used as a reducing supercritical fluid (Fig. 7.5 ). This method seems to be the most powerful approach at present.

Hydrothermal approaches have commonly been applied to synthesize GQDs and doped GQDs via bottom-up approaches. Numerous nonmetallic element-doped GQDs have been synthesized via the hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid as the carbon source in the presence of dicyandiamide [64], tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane [65], hydrazine [66], and cysteine [67]. The hydrothermal method can also be combined with microwaves to conveniently produce GQDs with uniform size distribution, since microwaves provide simultaneous, homogeneous, and fast heating. The GQDs obtained exhibit deep ultraviolet emission with the shortest emission wavelength among all the solution-based QDs with a PLQY from 7% to 11% (Fig. 7.6 ) [68].

A high yield of GQDs can be obtained by direct heating pyrolysis without any special equipment. An easy bottom-up method for the preparation of GQDs with 15nm in width and 0.5–2.0nm in thickness has been developed, by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid (Fig. 7.7 ) [69]. Interestingly, the GQDs show an excitation-independent PL emission with a PLQY of 9.0%, whereas the GO exhibits excitation-dependent PL emission [69]. Benefiting from the simplicity of this method, different GQDs can be synthesized by varying the precursors [70].

GQDs have abundant oxygen-containing groups including carbonyl, carboxyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl at their edges, which make them easy to be functionalized. Furthermore, the quantum confinement and edge effects in GQDs allow heteroatoms to dope directly into the carbon lattice and to modulate their electronic, optical, and chemical properties by creating more reactive sites [31–33].

The nitrogen atom has been the most widely used for doping carbon nanostructures owing to its comparable atomic size for bonding into carbon atoms. The larger electronegativity of the nitrogen atom than that of the carbon atom results in the polarization of the carbon network and thus influences the electronic and optical properties of the GQDs. There have been many reports on N-GQDs using various nitrogen sources including ammonia [71], glycine [70], dicyandiamide [64], and hydrazine [66]. Other nonmetallic elements dopants, such as boron [61,72–76], fluorine [77–79], phosphorus [63,80], sulfur [48,81–83], chloride [84], and selenium [85] have also been introduced for the fabrication of doped and codoped GQDs. Recently, a facile method for the fabrication of selenium-doped GQDs (Se-GQDs) using hydrothermal treatment of GQDs with NaHSe aqueous solution had been developed [85]. These Se-GQDs exhibit a strong yellow emission with a PLQY of 29% due to the n-π⁎ transition of C-Se. Se-GQDs show a reversible fluorescence switch in the detection of the oxidative hydroxyl radical (Fig. 7.8 ) and reductive glutathione.

In line with the boost progresses of nonmetallic element heteroatom-doped GQDs, interest in the metallic element doping with different atoms and their GQD performances has also been aroused. Up to now, the available metallic ion-doped GQDs include potassium-doped GQDs (K-GQDs) [86], europium-doped GQDs (Eu-GQDs) [51], and ferric-doped GQDs (Fe-GQDs) [87].

K-GQDs can be synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of sucrose and potassium hydroxide solution at 170°C for 4h [86]. The K-GQDs obtained have good crystallinity and an average diameter of 3.4nm and can be uniformly dispersed in water [51]. The results show that the introduction of K atoms creates surface functional groups on the GQDs and alters the energy level and structure of the GQDs. Besides, in order to broaden the bandgap of GQDs based on the strong negative inductive effect of Fe, Fe-GQDs have been synthesized using iron benzoate as a precursor [87]. As state of the art, these Fe-GQDs exhibit deep ultraviolet emission with a very high PLQY and possess good stability and low cytotoxicity.

In addition to hydrothermal methods, a strong acid-assisted reflux oxidation method has been designed to produce Eu-GQDs from Eu-doped 3D graphene [51]. Compared with Eu free GQDs, the introduction of Eu atoms enhances the electron density and improves the surface chemical activities of Eu-GQDs. The Eu-GQDs are used as fluorescent probes in the detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Functionalization of GQDs is another effective approach to modulate GQD properties by introducing different functional groups. Various strategies have been carried out to achieve functionalization. Compared with the passivation by polyethylene glycol (PEG) [88,89], aryl-modification [90], and other surface modification methods, the amidation method is the most extensively explored because of the large amount of carboxyl groups at the GQD edges. Generally, the amidation of carboxyl groups can be carried out through hydrothermal treatment using ammonia solution at 70–150°C [91,92]. The fluorescence emission of GQDs can be modulated under different reaction temperatures in the amidation of GQDs (af-GQDs, and the PLQY of af-GQDs is within 19%–29%) [54]. Owing to the excitation effect, the emission efficiency of the GQDs can be evidently increased to 46% when their surfaces are passivated with PEG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical Application of GQDs for the Determination of Metal Ions
ce:section.ce:para: It is very important to detect heavy metal ions in the environment. So far, a variety of GQD-based fluorescence sensors have been constructed for the sensitive and selective determination of metal ions, as listed in Table 7.1 .

As discussed above, some researchers attribute the mechanism for Fe3+ detection based on GQD fluorescence sensor to the special coordinate interaction between Fe3+ ion and phenolic hydroxyl groups of GQDs [93,94,100]. Besides, functionalized GQDs with heteroatom doping and amine groups have been used as efficient fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+. Nitrogen and sulfur codoped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) can be used as a sensitive fluorescence sensing material for Fe3+ detection with a wide linear range of 0–130mM and a low detection limit of 0.07mM [102]. N-GQDs and amino acid-functionalized GQDs also show a sensitive response to Fe3+ [66,99]. The authors indicate that the phenomenon is caused not only by the high thermodynamic affinity between Fe3+ and nitrogen atoms in the GQDs but also by the paramagnetism of Fe3+ and a similar detection mechanism has also been proposed [50]. It is also reported that the block copolymer-integrated GQDs (bcp-GQDs) can be used for the multifunctionally colorimetric sensing of temperature, pH, and metal ions, and the bcp-GQDs obtained show fast fluorescence quenching upon the addition of 100μM Fe3+ [98]. However, unlike the previously reported turnoff fluorescent nanosensor, a turn-on orange-red fluorescence nanosensor has been developed based on rhodamine B derivative-functionalized GQDs (RBD-GQDs) for the detection of Fe3+ in cancer stem cells [95]. In this work, a new strong peak accompanied by orange-red emission centered at 580nm appears with the addition of Fe3+ owing to the on-switch of the spirocyclic moiety in the RBD.

Apart from Fe3+, various GQDs have been produced that are selective and sensitive for the detection of Cu2+ [51,92,103]. A sensing approach based on amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) has been developed to detect Cu2+ [92]. The fluorescence quenching is caused by the facilitation of nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process, which is attributed to static and dynamic phenomena. An effective Cu2+ sensing approach using GQDs obtained by a hydrothermal method as a result of the formation of a complex between Cu2+ ions and the GQDs has been developed [103]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism appears to be predominantly of the static (rather than dynamic) type. Besides, the pristine GQDs could also be synthesized by the chemical oxidation of pitch graphite fibers. These GQDs could be easily and eco-friendly applied for the determination of Cu2+ in the water samples [104]. A novel “off-on” fluorescence probe for the label-free determination of Cu2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity using Eu-GQDs was recently reported [51]. An eco-friendly and simple route to prepare fluorescent GQDs functionalized by poly(ethylene imine) (PEI) has been proposed using coffee grounds as the precursor [105]. The PEI-GQDs are highly sensitive to Cu2+ ions as a fluorescent probe. The PL quenching may be caused by the irradiation combination of photoexcited electron-hole couples in the PEI-GQDs. As a result of the chelating interaction, a fast electron transfer process from the PEI-GQDs to Fe3+ or Cu2+ ions is inhibited [105].

Cr (VI) and Cd2+ are also the other main determination targets due to their high toxicity [112,115,117,120]. Lots of GQDs have been synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ with different sensitivity and selectivity [3,107,110]. In order to improve selectivity, the strategies of doping and functionalization of GQDs are frequently used. For example, GQDs modified with thymine-rich DNA (DNA-GQDs) improve the selectivity, and in the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescence of DNA-GQDs is quenched because the binding of Hg2+ to the thymine bases of the DNA reduces the electron transfer of DNA-GQDs [109]. Cysteine-doped GQDs (Cys-GQDs) can also be developed via a similar consideration [108]. On the basis that, in the presence of Hg2+, the aggregation of GQDs can be confirmed using dynamic light scattering and the aggregation causes the fluorescence quenching of GQDs, a highly selective “on-off” fluorescence sensing approach for the detection of Hg2+ was developed [106]. Since ratiometric fluorescence sensing can be easily observed with the naked eye without any sophisticated instrumentations or specially equipped laboratories, taking CdTe as the internal standard, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed using GQDs and a CdTe-based core-satellite hybrid for the detection of Hg2+ [112]. When the concentration of Hg2+ increases, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually decreases, and as shown in Fig. 7.9 , the test solution displays a vivid visual color change from blue to red with high selectivity and sensitivity (the detection limit being 3.3nM).

Using a hydrothermal method, 3,9-dithia-6-monoazaundecane (DMA)-functionalized GQDs (DMA-GQDs) were synthesized, with Pb2+ coordinating with the carboxylate groups of tryptophan and sulfur atoms on the surface of the DMA-GQDs through electrostatic interaction [116]. Because of the strong energy-transfer interactions between the tryptophan and the DMA-GQDs, the interaction causes the fluorescence enhancement of the DMA-GQDs-tryptophan compound system. The increase of the fluorescence intensity is linear to the concentration of Pb2+ in a wide range from 10−9 to 10−11 M, and the detection limit is 9pM. A highly selective and sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescence nanosensor for Pb2+ detection has been developed [117]. Recently, a microfluidic sample pretreatment device for Pb2+ preconcentration has been designed, equipped with a large-volume SPE chamber and a peristaltic pneumatic micropump for automatic sample loading and recovery [118]. In this approach, DNA aptamer functionalized GQDs work as the “on-off” sensing units for the detection of Pb2+ based on the electron transfer from GQDs to Pb2+, as illustrated in Fig. 7.10 , with the sensor exhibiting high sensitivity toward Pb2+ with a detection limit of 0.64nM and a linearity ranging from 1 to 1000nM. The on-chip preconcentration of the trace metal ions from a large-volume sample followed by metal ion detection using the fluorescence GQD sensor provides an advanced platform for on-site water pollution screening.

Recently, a novel GQD-based probe has been demonstrated for the detection of Ag+, with AgNPs generated on GQDs quenching the FL of GQDs by charge-transfer processes [119]. The protocol can also be applied for the selective detection of Au (III) [67,122]. However, the mechanism differs if the GQD type changes. Amine-functionalized GQDs (Am-GQDs) have been synthesized as an “on-off-on” probe for the selective detection of Ag+ based on Pearson's HSAB theory [120]. In Pearson's HSAB theory, soft acids react with soft bases faster and form stronger bonds, whereas hard acids react faster and form stronger bonds with hard bases. Based on the HSAB theory, S and Ag, which are soft base and soft acid, therefore form stronger bonds than those of the other metal ions (hard acids). Hence, Am-GQDs show selective recovery for Ag+ ions.

In aqueous media, Cr (VI) is ~100 times more toxic than Cr (III), and it is of significance to develop a sensitive and selective method for the detection of Cr (VI). N-GQDs for the detection of Cr (VI) with a wide linear range from 0 to 140 uM, and a detection limit of 40nM has been reported [115]. Most of the GQD-based fluorescence sensing processes are based on electron transfer: however, another mechanism for the detection of metal ions has aroused broad interest: the fluorescence N-GQDs are strongly quenched by Cr (VI) mainly from an inner filter effect (IFE) and a static quenching route, which result in the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system [115]. This approach shows sensitive responses to different concentration of Cr (VI) ranging from 0 to 140μM with a detection limit of 40nM, and it has been successfully used to selectively detect Cr (VI) in real samples [114].

The IFE mechanism promises a novel strategy for the sensing of metal ions. Ca2+ reacts with 5, 10, 15, 20-tetrakis (1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin, and this reaction can be accelerated using N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 7.11 . As a result, the spectra of porphyrin absorption and the N-GQD fluorescence change, owing to the IFE of porphyrins on the assembled N-GQDs. Both the absorption of porphyrins and the fluorescence signals can be used for the rapid and sensitive detection of Ca2+ in the range 0.5–8μM with a detection limit of 88nM [91].

In this chapter, we summarized the progress of synthesis, doping, and functionalization of GQDs as well as their application in the detection of metal ions. The roles of dopants and surface functional groups on the analytic performance of GQDs were also discussed. In addition, the detection mechanisms were discussed in order to extensively elicit more thought and constructive attempts toward metal ion sensing. Based on sensing studies and applications of GQDs, there are still some issues remaining for further studies:
(1)
Most PL properties of GQDs, such as the low QY and the wide half broadband of their fluorescence emission, are still not satisfactorily explained [31–33]. Therefore, it is very important to search for a steady method for the synthesis of GQDs with high QY, reproducibility, and narrow emission spectrum band.
(2)
Deep and sophisticated insight into the mechanisms for the PL of GQDs is very instructive for modulating luminescence [25]. This study would be quite beneficial for understanding their analytic performance. In addition, the mechanism studies for metal ion sensing are still ambiguous. A deeper understanding would help us to more effectively establish systems for the detection of metal ions.
(3)
The sensing targets are limited in several metal ions, such as Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+, which might be caused by the dopants being confined to only several elements, such as N, B, S, and Cl. Therefore, more dopants including metal ions should be investigated for modulating the properties of GQDs. Furthermore, the functionalization of GQDs also helps us to expand the determination of target ions, such as alkaline ions of Na+ and K+. Hence, how to propose a constructive strategy for effective functionalization should also be given more attention.
(4)
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing based on GQDs has been confirmed as a powerful tool for the determination of Hg2+ as illustrated above. More ratiometric fluorescence approaches need to be established to visualize the concentration of metal ions under certain UV illumination. These approaches will be useful for the determination of ions at the point of care without any sophisticated instruments.

Overall, we are still a long way from realizing the possible and satisfactory applications of GQDs. Using surface and bandgap modification via the synthesis of GQDs, surface functionalization, and heteroatom doping, we can expect to design and obtain novel materials for metal ion sensing with high selectivity and sensitivity.
